content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION

December 15, 1997


Stephen R. Ledoux
Mackenzie & Albritton
One Post Street, Suite 500
San Francisco, California 94104

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-97-527

Dear Mr. Ledoux:

This letter is in response to your request for advice regarding the provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹

QUESTION

May you act represent CellularOne on an ad hoc group organized by the City of Sausalito to study the telecommunications industry and revise proposed guidelines?

CONCLUSION

Yes. You may represent CellularOne on the ad hoc group.

FACTS

Paul Albritton of your firm Mackenzie & Albritton is the Mayor of Sausalito. Due to your firm's representation of CellularOne and other telecommunications clients, Mr. Albritton has recused himself from any decisions concerning individual cell site applications or the development of guidelines to regulate this industry.

At the suggestion of the planning commission and consensus approval of the city council (without Mr. Albritton and without a formal vote), the planning department has been asked to

¹ Government Code sections 81000 - 91014. Commission regulations appear at title 2, sections 18109 - 18995, of the California Code of Regulations.

428 J Street, Suite 450, Sacramento, California 95814 (916) 322-5660

File No. A-97-527
Page 2

organize an ad hoc group to study the industry and revise proposed guidelines which had been
prepared by staff. The guidelines thus revised would be presented to the planning commission
and the city council for final approval.

CellularOne has been asked to participate on the ad hoc group and has asked you to be
present due to your many years of experience working with local jurisdictions in your area. You
would not be a formal member of the ad hoc group, but would be present as CellularOne's
attorney and would offer your expertise in any area your client or the group desired. CellularOne
has an application on file in Sausalito which may be subject to the guidelines under review. You
understand that you should not represent the group in any presentation to the planning
commission or city council. It is anticipated that you will be made a partner in your firm this
year.

ANALYSIS

The Act prohibits any public official of any level of state or local government from
making, participating in making, or in any way attempting to use his or her official position to
influence a governmental decision in which he or she knows or has reason to know he or she has
a financial interest. (Section 87100.) Mr. Albritton, a public official, has decided to recuse
himself from any decision regarding CellularOne and other telecommunication clients, we
assume, at least partly, because of the prohibition found in Section 87100.² However, the Act
does not prohibit another member of a public official's firm from representing a client of the
public official's firm. (Smith Advice Letter, No. A-96-022; Rumansoff Advice Letter, No. I-94-
045.) Therefore, your presence on the ad hoc committee is not prohibited because of
Mr. Albritton's presumed conflict of interest.

However, our analysis does not end there. If you are a public official under the Act, then
Section 87100 may apply to you regardless of your professional relationship with Mr. Albritton.
According to the Act you may be a public official if you are a member of a board or commission
with decisionmaking authority. (Section 82048; Regulation 18700(a)(1).)

According to Regulation 18700(a)(1), a board or commission possesses decisionmaking
authority whenever:

"(A) It may make a final governmental decision; (B) It may
compel a governmental decision; it may prevent a governmental
decision either by reason of an exclusive power to initiate the
decision or by reason of a veto which may not be overridden; or

² You have not requested the Commission to advise or comment on Mr. Albritton's decision to recuse
himself from decisions affecting the telecommunication industry.

File No. A-97-527
Page 3

(C) It makes substantive recommendations which over an extended
period of time have been regularly approved without significant
amendment or modification by another public official or
governmental agency."

If the ad hoc group does not possess decisionmaking authority, you are not a public
official under the Act and you may serve as counsel and advocate to CellularOne during
discussions of the ad hoc group. (Section 82048.)³ While the facts suggest that the ad hoc group
does not possess decisionmaking authority, you have not provided sufficient facts regarding the
ad hoc group for us to make a conclusive determination on this issue. Also, please be aware that
under Regulation 18700(a)(1)(c), the ad hoc group may become a decisionmaking authority
sometime in the future.

If you are a public official you may not make, participate in making, or otherwise attempt
to influence governmental decisions which have a reasonably foreseeable and material financial
effect on any economic interest that you possess. Also, if you are a public official, Mr. Albritton
could not attempt to influence you regarding any decision in which he has an economic interest.

If you have any other questions regarding this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-
5660.

Sincerely,

Steven G. Churchwell
General Counsel

By:    Marte Castañios
Staff Counsel, Legal Division

SGC:MC:tls

³ A "public official" includes every member, officer, employee or consultant of a state or local
government agency. Your facts do not suggest that you are otherwise a public official under the Act.

I:\Advice Ltrs\97527.wpd

CONCLUSION: Yes. You may represent CellularOne on the ad hoc group.
